Breaking | Industry Update 📰

@seacrestmarkets has announced the closure of its prop trading programs, confirming a full transition toward operating solely as a CFD brokerage. The update was communicated directly to users via email, and no official announcement has been shared on the company’s social media channels at the time of writing.

Key points to note:

• Seacrest Markets is closing its prop trading programs, shifting focus entirely to its CFD brokerage
• No official update has been posted on social media despite emails being sent to users
• All Challenge accounts will be closed on February 6, 2026, at 12:00 PM GMT
• Traders with active Challenge accounts may request a refund of challenge fees until February 28, 2026
• Traders with funded accounts can request payouts for realized profits until February 28, 2026
• All accounts are now close-only, with open positions force-closed at the February 6 deadline
• Traders who have never received a payout may also request a full refund by February 28, 2026
• Refunds or payouts can be transferred into a Seacrest CFD brokerage account, including an incentive of bonus trading credit (up to $500)
• The CFD brokerage will continue offering Forex, Commodities, Indices, and Cryptocurrencies via MT5

We will continue monitoring for any public statements or additional clarification and share confirmed updates as they emerge.

result.text:
BREAKING: UK Law Now Officially Recognises Digital Assets 🇬🇧 

The UK has today taken an important step forward in recognising the role of digital assets in the modern economy. A new law has come into effect confirming that qualifying digital assets — including crypto-tokens, stablecoins and NFTs — can be treated as property under UK law.

This change provides greater clarity and protection for consumers and investors by ensuring that digital assets can be clearly owned, recovered in cases of theft or fraud, and included within insolvency and estate processes. It marks a meaningful shift towards giving everyday holders the same confidence and certainty they expect with other forms of property.

Crucially, this development also strengthens the foundations for future innovation across the UK’s digital asset and tokenisation landscape. By providing a clear legal basis for ownership and transfer, the UK is now better positioned to support the growth of new financial products, tokenised real-world assets, and more secure digital markets. These legal protections help create the certainty businesses need to build responsibly and invest in long-term, consumer-focused innovation.

CryptoUK has consistently advocated for a clear and robust legal framework for digital assets, and we are pleased to see this important progress. We look forward to continuing our work with policymakers and industry to support the development of a competitive, responsible and future-ready UK market.

result.text:
📰 BREAKING | INDUSTRY WATCH

Automation in Textile Manufacturing Goes Viral — What the Videos Show, and What They Leave Out

Viral clips of near-empty textile factories are spreading fast, fuelling claims that automation has already wiped out human labour. The visuals are real. The conclusions are not. Here’s a fact-first breakdown of what’s actually happening inside modern textile manufacturing.

🔴 WHAT’S CONFIRMED
•Automation is accelerating globally, led by China and other major manufacturing hubs.
•AI, robotics, sensors, and smart systems are now standard in high-volume textile plants.
•Viral videos showing few or no workers on the shop floor are authentic.
•These facilities operate under Industry 4.0 models—continuous, data-driven production.

🟠 “UNMANNED FACTORIES” — REAL, BUT LIMITED
•Some plants run with minimal visible labour, mainly in:
•Weaving and spinning
•Fabric cutting
•Dyeing and finishing
•Humans haven’t disappeared—they’ve moved off the floor:
•Maintenance engineers
•Control-room operators
•Automation and software technicians
•Quality and logistics staff
•Routine operator roles are shrinking; the system is not human-free.

🟡 WHAT MACHINES REPLACE — AND WHAT THEY DON’T

Highly automatable
•Weaving, spinning
•Cutting
•Dyeing/finishing
•AI-based visual inspection

Still human-dependent
•Complex stitching
•Design changes
•Custom garments
•Small-batch and artisanal work

🟢 JOB IMPACT: DISPLACEMENT VS TRANSFORMATION
•Low-skill, repetitive roles are declining where automation scales fast.
•Output is rising; employment is stagnant or falling in some regions.
•New jobs are growing in:
•Machine maintenance
•Systems monitoring
•Data and industrial IT
•Robotics and automation support
•Risk is highest where reskilling and transition support are weak.

⚠️ WHY THESE VIDEOS GO VIRAL
•Empty floors create high-impact visuals.
•Short clips omit context and are often:
•Promotional showcases
•Selectively framed
•Misread as industry-wide norms
•One factory ≠ the entire global textile sector.

❌ MYTHS VS ✔️ REALITY
•❌ “Automation means no human jobs”
•❌ “AI has already erased textile employment”
•❌ “One viral plant represents the whole industry”
•✔️ Automation reduces some roles, shifts others
•✔️ Most factories run human–machine collaboration

📌 WHAT REALLY MATTERS NEXT
•Automation is not optional for competitiveness.
•The decisive factors now are:
•Worker reskilling
•Education aligned to industrial change
•Policy planning for labour transitions
•Countries that prepare workers adapt.
•Those that don’t face inequality—not instant joblessness.

🔚 BOTTOM LINE
•The machines are real.
•The transformation is real.
•The claim that AI has already ended human work is not.

Automation isn’t the end of jobs.
It’s the end of old job definitions.
What happens next depends on people—not machines.

#Automation
#Manufacturing
#FutureOfWork

BREAKING:

Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi decides to ease the country’s restrictive rules on arms exports.

The government has announced that the previous ban on exports had to be relaxed in order to stimulate the domestic defense industry.

The export of some defensive equipment, such as mine sweepers and radars, has been allowed since Shinzo Abe changed the law in 2014 but the new guidelines will make it possible for allied countries to buy offensive weapons such fighter jets, cruise missiles and warships.

Japan’s decision is great news for its allies in North America and Europe which have depleted weapon stocks after the wars in Ukraine and Iran, with their industrial capacity struggling to keep up with the demand for new short-term arms production

2026 Integrated Packer & Cold Storage Report

Reporting Period: March 13, 2026 (Q1 Performance Update)

Breaking Industry News

Packer & Processing News

Packer Margins Turn Positive: In a major reversal from February's record losses, average beef packer margins turned positive this week, reaching +$20.00 per head. This recovery was driven by a sharp rise in the Choice Boxed Beef Cutout to a six-month high of $397.09/cwt.

The "Family Grocery and Farmer Relief Act": Introduced in the Senate on March 5, 2026, this landmark legislation proposes a "Single Protein Mandate." If passed, it would force giants like Tyson and Cargill to divest operations so they only process one species (e.g., only beef OR only pork).

Foreign Ownership Ban: The new bill specifically targets foreign-controlled firms like JBS S.A. and Smithfield Foods, potentially requiring them to sell their U.S. assets to domestic companies within a strict timeframe.

Labor Alert: A potential strike at the JBS Greeley beef plant (Colorado) is currently being monitored, with workers authorizing a walkout that could disrupt 5% of national daily slaughter capacity.
Cold Storage & Inventory News

USDA Inventory Tightening: Total red meat in cold storage has fallen to 863M lbs, down 2% year-over-year.

Beef Storage Floor: Beef stocks fell to 435M lbs (-4% YoY). Boneless beef (grinding material) saw the steepest drop, falling 5% as retailers pull from reserves to maintain ground beef supplies.

Pork Belly Build: Pork bellies in storage surged 16% month-over-month, now sitting 3% higher than last year. This "bacon buffer" is expected to cap retail price spikes through the spring.

The Butter Rally: After a 17% YoY deficit in storage, wholesale butter prices have "gone vertical," rallying from $1.40 to $1.70 per lb in just 30 days.

Sector-Specific Performance Analysis

Beef & Cattle: The Supply Chokehold

The cattle herd is at its smallest since 1951, creating an unprecedented supply floor.

Slaughter Rates: Capacity utilization has dipped to 74.7%, as packers intentionally slow lines to maintain the newfound positive margins.

Carcass Weights: Weights remain record-high at 895 lbs (+27 lbs YoY), a necessary strategy to wring more "total pounds" out of fewer animals.

Import Strategy: The USDA has raised the 2026 import forecast to 5.57 billion lbs to meet the demand for lean processing beef.

Pork & Hogs: The Efficiency Leader
Pork production is currently the industry's primary volume driver.

Production Forecast: 2026 is on track for 28.2 billion lbs (+2.3% YoY).

Inventory: Unlike beef, pork stocks are healthy, with hams (+6%) and butts (+38%) seeing significant builds.

Market Sentiment: Hogs are trading at a "value" discount to cattle, making pork the preferred protein for feature-based retail ads.
Poultry: The Inflation Hedge
Chicken: Broiler production is being adjusted higher to meet "substitution demand" from beef-weary consumers.

Turkey Scarcity: Turkey remains the outlier in poultry; storage is 8% lower than 2025 due to lingering HPAI (Bird Flu) production gaps.

Retail Move: "Dark meat" chicken (thighs/legs) is seeing record velocity in the "Family Pack" segment.

Dairy: The Protein Power Move
Output: Total milk production is forecast at 234.5 billion lbs, driven by record "Milk per Cow" yields despite a shrinking national herd.

Powder Surge: Non-fat dry milk prices have spiked from $1.20 to $1.40 in three weeks, driven by the "Food as Medicine" movement and high demand for dairy-based proteins.

Executive Data Summary
Beef: Stock-Critically Tight, YOY Change-Down 4%, Packer Margin-Positive ($20/hd), Outlook-Bullish (Supply)

Pork: Stock-Building, YOY Change-Up 1%, Packer Margin-Stable, Outlook-Expansion

Poultry: Stock-Steady, YOY Change-Down 3%, Packer Margin-Positive, Outlook-Steady/Growth

Dairy: Stock-Tight (Butter), YOY Change-Down 17% (Butter), Packer Margin-Improving, Outlook-High Yield

Strategic Outlook
The Divestiture Risk (Packers)
The proposed "Single Protein" legislation creates an immediate need for "Antitrust Audits."

Strategy: Multi-species packers should begin evaluating which protein lines are "core" and which would be the most attractive for potential spin-offs or sales to meet domestic ownership requirements.

The "Bacon-as-Value" Play (Retailers)

Strategy: With pork belly storage up 16%, retailers have a massive window to use bacon as a "loss leader" to drive foot traffic, offsetting the sticker shock of $10+/lb ground beef.

Inventory Hedge (Cold Storage)

Strategy: Given the JBS Greeley strike threat, buyers should prioritize securing an additional 7-10 days of beef inventory immediately. A plant closure of that scale would instantly erase the current "positive" margin gains and spike spot-market prices.

$RGR Q4 2025 earnings: Top-Line Resilience Masks Severe Profitability Deterioration

Ruger proved it can still drive sales in a contracting market, but it is paying a heavy price on the bottom line. Q4 net sales grew 3.6% YoY to $151.1M, breaking industry stagnation despite a 4.1% annual drop in adjusted NICS background checks. However, the volume did not translate to profit. Q4 Adjusted EPS collapsed 58% to $0.26, and the company posted a full-year GAAP net loss of $4.4M. While aggressive destocking and 65 new product models position the top line well, the integration of the Anderson facility and higher operational costs crushed margins. Management explicitly acknowledged that improving the bottom line and aligning manufacturing capacity is now 'essential.'

result.text:
DeFi does not lack yield. It lacks risk infrastructure.

The industry has spent years optimizing for returns. Higher APYs. Complex strategies. The assumption: if you can generate yield, capital will follow.

This assumption is breaking down since fancy yield metrics carry risks most don't account for.

Here's how we do due diligence at Clearstar: 
https://t.co/4u1F5O9yr1

Capital at scale behaves differently than capital at the margin. A retail user chasing 50% APY and an institution allocating $10M or more have fundamentally different requirements. The institution needs to answer questions that the APY number cannot address:

What is the maximum drawdown scenario? What is the liquidity profile if we need to exit? Who is on the other side of this?

Most DeFi protocols are not built to answer them. Clearstar exists at this layer. We create the infrastructure that makes yield deployable at institutional scale.

This means:
- Continuous monitoring
- Exposure limits and concentration controls
- Documented risk parameters with clear rationale
- Transparent reporting on what is actually happening on-chain

Yield attracts capital. Risk infrastructure retains it.

Remarkable Strides in the Aviation Industry
Ghanaian women are increasingly breaking barriers and making significant strides in the aviation sector, with many boldly pursuing careers as pilots.

A shining example is 24-year-old Hamida Fuseini, who hails from Yendi-Adibo in the Northern Region and currently resides in Tamale. After completing her secondary education at Nobisco, she pursued her lifelong dream of becoming a pilot.

Through sheer hard work, unwavering determination, and remarkable courage, Hamida has etched her name in history as the first female pilot from Northern Ghana to obtain a Private Pilot License (PPL), a Commercial Pilot License (CPL), and a Multi-Engine Instrument Rating (MEIR).

result.text:
The Spring 2026 ACI Concrete Convention had record-breaking attendance, which included industry professionals from over 40 different countries.🌟 The international efforts at each convention reflect ACI’s continued work to increase global participation and disseminate industry knowledge worldwide.

🌎 International activities at the Spring Convention included the ACI International Forum, International Lunch, International Session: "Carbon Conscious Concrete – International Efforts for CO2 Reduction in the Concrete Industry," and the International Speakers Committee Meeting.

🌎 The ACI international team convened with distinguished representatives from three key global regions—Latin America, Western Europe, and Asia Pacific—highlighting the organization’s continued commitment to international collaboration. During these meetings, ACI signed memorandums of understanding (MOUs) with FIB (The International Federation for Structural Concrete), IBRACON (Instituto Brasileiro Do Concreto), and Associação Brasileira de Normas Técnicas (ABNT). This latest collaboration adds to ACI’s expanding portfolio of international relationships, now numbering more than 75 organizations.

🌎 The FRP Composites Student Competition featured 43 teams from around the world, including the USA, Bangladesh, Canada, Mexico, Ecuador, Peru, Poland, Guatemala, Columbia, the Philippines, and Puerto Rico.

🌎 ACI also recognized a distinguished group of Chapters from around the world with Excellent and Outstanding ACI Chapters. Winners included chapters from the USA, India, Mexico, Canada, the Philippines, Singapore, Peru and the Republic of Columbia.

For more information on specific concrete resources available for your region, visit the ACI regional portals in the top left corner at https://t.co/OryDLIGg6t.
